rationale_for_baseline_tier: This is an acute SEVERE colitis flare with systemic toxicity (subjective fever with measured 37.8, tachycardia HR 112, borderline hypotension 105/65), ongoing visible rectal bleeding, unintentional weight loss and early volume depletion, in a patient living alone who has recently taken an anti-motility agent. Telehealth cannot examine the abdomen or obtain the bloods and imaging needed to exclude an evolving toxic megacolon or perforation. A systemically-toxic, volume-depleted patient with ongoing GI bleeding needs same-day Emergency-Department-level assessment (bloods, IV fluids, abdominal imaging, specialist review) — T4. Escalation to T5/000 applies immediately if any surgical-abdomen or shock feature appears. T3 (urgent same-day in-person assessment) is the minimum acceptable answer; anything less under-triages a severe attack. This is NOT a low-acuity functional presentation — the source Risk Level is not 'Low', the disposition was urgent (not routine), and genuine alarm features are present.

escalation_edges.trigger_sign_or_symptom: Sudden, constant, unbearably sharp abdominal pain, or the abdomen becoming hard and rigid like a board
escalation_edges.rationale: Indicates peritonitis from perforation of the inflamed bowel — a surgical emergency needing an ambulance now.
escalation_edges.communication_template: If your belly suddenly becomes constantly, unbearably painful or goes hard and rigid like a board, call 000 straight away — this could mean the bowel has perforated.

escalation_edges.trigger_sign_or_symptom: Complete cessation of bowel movements together with a swelling/distending belly and ongoing severe pain
escalation_edges.rationale: Suggests toxic megacolon or obstruction — a life-threatening complication, its risk raised by the recent loperamide use; needs emergency hospital care.
escalation_edges.communication_template: If your belly starts to swell up and you stop passing motions and wind while the pain continues, call 000 — the bowel may be dangerously distended.

escalation_edges.trigger_sign_or_symptom: Fainting or collapse, near-collapse, or feeling you are about to pass out
escalation_edges.time_bound_condition: Immediately — call 000; lie down with legs raised while waiting
escalation_edges.rationale: Signals hypovolaemic shock from fluid and blood loss — an emergency, and the patient lives alone.
escalation_edges.communication_template: If you faint or feel you are about to collapse, call 000, lie down and raise your legs — you may be losing too much fluid and blood.

escalation_edges.trigger_sign_or_symptom: Vomiting blood, much heavier or more frequent bleeding, or a high fever with shaking chills (rigors)
escalation_edges.time_bound_condition: Same day — go to the Emergency Department
escalation_edges.rationale: Marks worsening bleeding or systemic sepsis needing urgent hospital assessment and resuscitation.
escalation_edges.communication_template: If you vomit blood, the bleeding gets much heavier, or you spike a high fever with shaking chills, go to the Emergency Department the same day.

contextual_modifiers.patient_factor: Lives alone with no one present to help or monitor, and is worried about fainting
contextual_modifiers.effect_on_advice: Lower the threshold to escalate: arrange transport that does not rely on the patient driving, ask whether a family member/friend/neighbour can attend or check on them, and prefer ambulance (000) if faintness makes safe self-transport doubtful.

contextual_modifiers.patient_factor: Recent self-administration of an anti-motility agent (loperamide) during an active flare
contextual_modifiers.effect_on_advice: Explicitly instruct the patient to stop it and take no more, and heighten vigilance for toxic-megacolon features (swelling belly, cessation of bowel movements).

baseline_safety_net_advice.default_tier_advice: You need to be seen in person today — go to the Emergency Department (or arrange urgent same-day assessment) for blood tests, fluids and a check of your tummy, because this level of illness cannot be safely assessed over the phone. Stop the Imodium/loperamide now and take no more anti-diarrhoea medicine. Keep sipping small amounts of electrolyte fluids. Call 000 immediately if your belly becomes hard or swollen, the pain becomes constant and unbearable, you stop passing anything, you feel faint or collapse, or you vomit blood.
baseline_safety_net_advice.contact_method: Attend the Emergency Department today; call 000 for an ambulance if any emergency feature appears or if you feel too faint to travel safely
baseline_safety_net_advice.after_hours_advice: The Emergency Department and 000 are available 24 hours — this is urgent and must not wait for clinic hours or a routine GP appointment.
baseline_safety_net_advice.documentation_for_gp: Provide the GP/ED with the timeline (3 months of diarrhoea, 48-hour deterioration with blood and mucus), the 6 kg weight loss, the systemic features (subjective fever, palpitations), the recent loperamide use, and the pending investigations (stool MCS/C. difficile toxin, faecal calprotectin, FBC, CRP) plus the need for an urgent gastroenterology referral.

critical_under_triage.failure_description: Treating a systemically-toxic acute severe colitis flare as a self-limiting gastroenteritis or functional bowel disorder suitable for self-care or routine/prompt GP review.
critical_under_triage.patient_harm_scenario: Unmonitored progression to toxic megacolon, perforation or hypovolaemic collapse in a patient living alone, with the delay potentially fatal.

serious_under_triage.failure_description: Arranging urgent same-day in-person assessment but at a setting that cannot deliver bloods, IV fluids and imaging to exclude toxic megacolon; acceptable as a minimum only if clear ED-level escalation triggers are given.

moderate_over_triage.failure_description: Calling an emergency ambulance for a patient who could, with escalation safeguards, self-present to the ED — defensible given systemic toxicity and living alone, and far preferable to under-triage.

clinical_uncertainty_handling.key_uncertainty: Whether an evolving toxic megacolon or perforation is present, and whether a superimposed infection (e.g. C. difficile) is contributing — none of which can be determined via telehealth.
clinical_uncertainty_handling.correct_response_to_uncertainty: Escalate to urgent in-person/ED assessment, name the exact features that mandate 000, and stop the anti-motility agent — act on the worst plausible diagnosis while it cannot be excluded.
clinical_uncertainty_handling.wrong_response_to_uncertainty: Reassuring the patient it is likely a viral gastroenteritis or IBS, endorsing loperamide to control symptoms, or deferring to a routine GP appointment.
